moment_text: And so finally. He ended up going to this school. It was a country day school. What they told us is they taught to interest and so we thought, okay, that's what would work for him. moment_text: I thought A DHD meant that you're all over the place. You're not very smart, and you're a problem. And I, I wouldn't have said that was my son at all. She had three meetings with, one was with our family, one was just with my son and one was with my husband and me. She came back and she told us that our job as his parents was to reduce his expectations because he was far too ambitious, I guess, for her liking, and she thought. He's gonna be disappointed. moment_text: I think that for too long, a DHD has been framed as a deficit, a disorder that needs fixing. I've never bought that. I don't buy that. By the way, there is actually a study out of Canada. No one ever talks about it, and it was a very large study that showed that 43% of people with A DHD are actually in excellent mental health. And so my job is to teach women to show women. That they're not disordered. They're just different. Much like Max in a Windows driven world who we just need a different manual and. I know that the A DHD brain comes with incredible strengths. I mean, I have literally worked with thousands of women with A DHD, and I've never met a one that wasn't truly brilliant at something. And so what I also know is when we stop focusing on what's wrong with us and start looking at what's right, everything changes. That's because, you know, whatever you focus on grows, right? It doesn't matter if it's, you know, the, the things that cause positive emotion or the things that cause negative emotion. So my thought is let's focus on the things that generate positive emotion. Because positive emotion actually helps us create dopamine, more dopamine in our brains, which is what we don't make enough of. So I think that shift alone. Of looking at the strengths of A DHD, it reduces shame, it reduces self-doubt, it gets rid of imposter syndrome, and those are the exact things that I believe crush mental wellness, certainly for A DHD brains, but literally for every brain.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains strong barrier, agency, and emotional signals within a system interaction (healthcare_provider).

moment_text: I, what I do is foundational work. I really believe that if you are on the highest tree. Who gives a damn if you're in the wrong forest? And I think so many women. Positive emotion generates dopamine, so you're much more likely to then do the next step and the step after that. moment_text: I guess what I would say is, and this is for everyone, A DHD or not, you are the expert on you do not abdicate your mental health to anyone. Doctors do not know more than you about what works for you, and the simple reason, I mean, they may know all the medical stuff, right. But bottom line, you are the only one that has that rudder inside yourself. You're the only one that has access to what actually makes you feel good.

moment_text: What a lot of women will do, they'll be prescribed medication, whether it's for anxiety, depression or A DHD. They start taking it and they feel worse and worse. But because the doctor said this is what you should take, they keep taking it and they literally lose. Track and sight of what it actually feels like to feel good so your doctor doesn't have access to that rudder, that thermometer inside you that tells you, okay, I'm feeling positive emotion versus negative emotion. So I really. I've met so many women that were on anxiety medication or depression medication for decades, and it never got better. And then lo and behold, they discover that, oh wait. It wasn't actually anxiety and depression at all, or underlay at all, was a DHD. And so when you treat the A DHD, the anxiety and depression also resolve, or at a minimum gets so much better.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is rich with barrier signals (misdiagnosis, delayed_diagnosis) and describes a clear interaction with the healthcare system, making it highly eligible for insight promotion.

moment_text: I don't care who you are. Focus on your strengths, not your weaknesses. I mean, if you think about it, has anybody moved society, moved the world, achieved greatness in their weaknesses? No. Focus on your strengths, because that's where all the positive emotion is. That's where the dopamine and the motivation and everything you need to be successful are. But I think as a society, you know, certainly in school it's always about like trying to get better at what you. Just naturally are not good at, and I think that's the wrong message.
